disclaimer: FIXTURE_ID=transcript_grief. This transcript is fictional. All content synthesized for evaluation purposes only and does not represent any real clinical encounter or patient.
full_text: Patient is grieving the death of their mother three months ago. Session explored waves of grief, ambivalent feelings about sorting belongings, and the utility of grief journaling between sessions. Therapist introduced continuing bonds framing and offered a journaling prompt for the week.

segments.text: It's been three months since my mom died and it still knocks me over sometimes. Yesterday I smelled her perfume in a store and I had to sit down in the parking lot for twenty minutes.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Those ambushes are one of the hardest parts of grief. You're doing your day and then suddenly you're not.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Exactly. And it's been wave after wave — I'll feel okay for a day or two, then it crashes back.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: The waves pattern you're describing is really characteristic of how grief actually moves — not a straight line, not stages.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: My brother keeps pushing me to sort through her apartment and I just can't. I opened one drawer last weekend and had to close it.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: What comes up for you when you think about her belongings?
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: It's like — if I sort her things, then I'm admitting she's really gone. I know that's not rational.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: It's not irrational at all. Her things are a tangible continuing connection. There's a concept called continuing bonds — the idea that grief isn't about letting go of the person, it's about finding a new form of the relationship that can go forward with you.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: I like that. Continuing bonds. That's gentler than the 'acceptance' framing.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Have you ever tried writing to her? A letter, or a journal entry addressed to her?
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: No. It feels weird. But also maybe comforting, I don't know.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: The ambivalence itself is information. I'd like to offer a prompt for the week if you're open — a short grief journal entry, addressed to your mother, three times this week. Five minutes each, no pressure on content.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Three entries this week, five minutes each. Okay. That feels doable.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: And the apartment — is it okay to put a pin in that? Your brother can wait.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Yeah. I'll tell him I need more time. That's actually a relief to say out loud.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Good. And how has sleep been the last few weeks?
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Broken. I wake up at three or four most nights. Sometimes I cry, sometimes I just lie there.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: I hear you. We can revisit sleep specifically next session if the broken sleep pattern persists.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Thanks. I think I just needed permission to be not okay this week.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: You have that permission. Every week, until it doesn't feel like a burden.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

test_queries.query: What did we talk about regarding grief and my mom's belongings?
What journaling exercise did the therapist suggest?
